speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May the 10th, 1996. The Shoah Foundation is interviewing Zangwill Gilbert. The interviewer is Norman Pell. We are in Los Angeles, California, USA. The interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 10, 1996. We are in Los Angeles, California to interview Mr. Zangwill Gilbert. The interview will be conducted in English. My name is Norman Pell. Good morning, Mr. Gilbert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, to you, Mr. Pell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we start by you giving us your full name and let us know when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Zangwill Gilbert. I was born in Newcastle on Tyne, September the 11th, 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Newcastle on Tyne is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is in England, the northern-- northeastern part of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother came over from Poland. My father was from [POLISH]. My mother was from Lodz. And they must have got-- my-- my father-- they got married about two years after he finished his military service. He was what is called in Poland, [POLISH] in '95, the year '95. They came to England in early 1911. My father was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. My mother was a housewife, nothing but. And I am the fifth child that was born out of nine children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us first, what was dad's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yaakov Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gilbert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yaakov Sheldon Gelbat, but they called it Gilbert in England. And my mother's name was Miriam Ulstein, which was the Polish name, Ulstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, Lou, [INAUDIBLE], he was born in 1903. My-- [INAUDIBLE]. My second brother was Schulem, Chaim Mendel, Manny, was born in 1905. My sister, Shifra [? Raizel ?], who is still alive and is 89 years old, she was born also-- all-- they were all born in Poland-- she was born in 1907. Now, my next sister, Blima, she was born in 1909. She lives here in Los Angeles. The-- the address is up there, in case you-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: She was-- she was also born in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. And then, I was born in England by a magic touch. My mother said to my father, Yaakov Schulem, [NON-ENGLISH] because we cannot [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't understand what they-- And the time that my mother took to go, the North Sea started to act up. So it was delayed. So by the time they told her, you could now move, I started to kick while I was on the train, and I was born in Newcastle. Therefore I am a British citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family, at the time, was living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Living in London. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, mother wanted-- when you say it was an accident-- she wanted to go back where?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to go back to Lodz where her family lived in number 25 [POLISH] In Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So she was delayed, and so you were born in Newcastle-

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the delay.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and became a British subject.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and [NON-ENGLISH] in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were brought up in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brought up in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. After you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, after me comes my sister, Nita. Yeah. My sister, Hanna, who called herself Nita. That was the one I told you married a Belgian boy by the name of Leo Van West, and her husband was taken away to the concentration camp at Sachsenhausen. And she escaped during that. She escaped being taken away, and my niece, her daughter Michelle-- who today, in New York, is a doctor. And she married again in New York. And she married a man with the name of-- a guy by the name of Cooperman, and her son, who is named after my father, is a dental surgeon doing very well. After Hanna, came [? Suma ?], Sid. He lives in London at this time. He was born Christmas Eve 1916. After him, the war finished-- World War I finished in 1918. And in 1919, my sister, Zelda, Sylvia, was born. And she died at the age-- she was born in 1919. And she died at the age of 69, dropped dead outside a supermarket in London. After her, there's my brother Herschel Zvi, who lives in Wilmette, Chicago. And in the wintertime, he lives in Boca Raton in Florida. Apart from that family of nine children, we also had added to-- my mothers hard work-- we also had two young men from the [INAUDIBLE], which is a Jewish orphanage home in west Norwood in London. They came and lived with us because they were apprenticed, and they were told to train to [NON-ENGLISH]. To me, they're like brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] being tailoring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailoring, yes. And that-- that-- that is the, more or less [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, what was life like in England at the time you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, where we were living in Whitechapel, Stepney, it was a solid Jewish neighborhood. And the school that I went to was Myrtle Street School. And there was only one Gentile boy there, whose name was Johnny Abbot. And I have pictures to prove it, of school pictures that-- that when-- that-- And we went to school until we were 14. And I had a wonderful headmaster and master by the name of Mr. Hadida. He was a Sephardic gentleman. And whilst I went to school, I was studying music at the Guildhall School of Music. And I had to go once a week for my lesson. And he used to leave-- the school used to close at 4 o'clock, you know, the day. But he used to leave me go at 3:00 so that I could change my clothes and put my best on to take the bus to go to John Carpenter Street, which is in Blackfriars, where the Guildhall School of Music was.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] Tell us-- tell us a little about what life at home was like when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew-- my mother was very, very frum. Frum, but my father was not. And there were quite a-- a few amusing anecdotes. If I was caught whistling in the house on a Saturday, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, my mother used to give it to me. And every Friday, I had to go to shul. And she used to smell my breath first to find out if I-- all the young boys used to go up, and after the kiddish, everyone took a sip of wine, in Fieldgate Street Shul. And every week, she used to ask me, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What prayers did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a certain [NON-ENGLISH] there every week, you see? And one day-- it's very amusing. You see, the workers that used to work for my father-- I used to go to the bank to get the money to pay wages, and stamps, the unemployment and health insurance stamps. And when I used to pay the people-- and what was left was the profit for my father, and I used to give it to him. Then after they'd gone-- they used to-- Monday through to Saturday half day. And on that Saturday, after Shabbat, the table was laid for the Shabbos [NON-ENGLISH] lunch. And my father used to say, Miriam, [NON-ENGLISH]. So I used-- I said to my mother once, Mother, you hit me when I whistle because you say [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't whistle on--

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't whistle on Saturday. But Dad gave you money. What are you taking money on Saturday, if you're so frum? And my mother answered me, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you could translate that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Translate-- what is to [INTERPOSING VOICES] God is to God. And what is to the people is to the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, your-- your-- your home life was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the kids went--

speaker: interviewee
text: We know when there was a Yom Tov. We used to polish up everything. All my sisters, everyone worked scrubbing. And especially when it came Pesach. The [NON-ENGLISH] was-- the [NON-ENGLISH] is the-- the cups and saucers-- change them, everything. And [NON-ENGLISH] for the guy to come around that used-- two boys used to be swinging a fire, you know, and say, [NON-ENGLISH] and you gave them a penny. And you put the [YIDDISH] and the [YIDDISH] in, and little things like that. Yes. It was very, very Yiddish. And there was a Yiddish theater there and everything. And then, after I came out of school, I won a scholarship. Before I left school, I won my first scholarship, then I won a year's free tuition at the Royal Academy-- another scholarship and the violin was given to me-- and I won a year's free tuition at the Royal Academy of Music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was where? in-- in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London. Everything was in London. And then, I got a job. And this was playing in a cinema during the silence. And everything was going--

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was that, when you got your first job?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1920-- November 1925. I left school in April 1925, and I got my first job. And I was earning very good money, you see? And I went on until the talkies started to come in. And I saw that, in spite of what everybody was saying, this was another thing that was happening. The people would say, the musicians that used to be from Archer Street [INAUDIBLE]. It'll never work. It's canned music, and all that. I know. My father, you see, is a [NON-ENGLISH]. And when I had a moment, I always had to go up into the workshop, pulling bastings and things like that, and learning how to sew. I think I showed you the other day the thimble that I had. When my father put that thimble on my finger, I had to walk around the workshop with my finger like that, showing everybody that the [NON-ENGLISH], you see. Anyhow, my father wanted me to go into the workshop. And I said, uh-uh. He wanted me to be a [NON-ENGLISH], and I said, I don't want-- he says, if you don't want to be a [NON-ENGLISH], you'd better find yourself a job quickly. And I went to the West End, which is the-- like, the downtown of-- of-- of London, the West End. I gave an audition. I had no money in my pocket.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave an audition, and the man said, can you start tonight? And I said, yes. So he said, I'll give you a pound-- that was 20 shillings at that time. And he said, do you have a black bow? I said no. He said Stag and Russell's, just around the corner, there. They're still-- go and buy a black bow. Go and have something to eat. And then go down to Southampton to meet the the ship called the the Royal Mail ship

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sailed. And I ate, like nobody's business, the crew food before we sailed. And we went from Southampton to Le Havre, from Le Havre to Vigo in Spain, from Vigo to Lisbon, from Lisbon to the Canary Islands, from the Canary Islands right across the southern Atlantic to Santos, Santos to Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro to Montevideo in Uruguay, and then we went to Buenos Aires, where I have cousins that were waiting for me because I had written to them that I was coming, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a passenger cruise ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was a passenger ship, period-- a passenger-- a-- a-- a cargo, you see. But when I say a passenger ship, it was a 28,000 tonner, so it was not a small ship. And we docked in Buenos Aires at the [SPANISH] del Norte. That's the north-- the north bay, and number seven, dock number-- [SPANISH]. And I spent 10 days there. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do on the ship? Were you part of an-- a-- an orchestra?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orchestra. Playing violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Playing the violin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Surely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. And I made a nice few pounds. I did very, very well. And then a telegram came from the agent on my second voyage for me to transfer to the White Star liner And I transferred to the and came to New York. I sailed from Southampton to New York. After a while, I got a bit tired of it, you know. After a while, then I decided to find a job. It was very difficult, because in the dance band business-- I'd now learned how to play dance music, because before, I never played dance music, you see. I learned to play dance music. And there was a band coming over from the Ambassador Hotel here in Los Angeles called Eddie Oliver. And that was the time of Prohibition. And when the ship landed-- his ship landed in England-- two of the guys had to be sent back in a basket from drinking, you know. And he came looking for two men to join the band. And we opened up at the Commodore Hotel in the Boulevard Haussmann in Paris, Eddie at the Ambassador's Hotel-- it was supposed to be a copy of the Coconut Grove here. And from there, I kept moving around in Europe. I worked in Germany. I worked in Italy. I-- I coming home all the time. And that's when I-- when I came home and got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 1934, November 1934. I was very much in love with her. To me-- and the picture that you got-- she was one of the most beautiful girls that you-- anyone could see. She was a real head turner. And I felt very, very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with a friend to a club, and there I met her. It was a-- it was a Jewish club, you know, for the young people, you see. And there I met her. And we got married March the 15th. Sunday, March the 15th, 1936, we got married. But of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: In London?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London at-- with Rabbi Shaposhnik's synagogue, which was in Bedford Square in London. Everything I'm talking about, at this point, is London. Now, from there-- you know, a traveling musician's life is like a sailor. A sailor goes away and comes back and sends money home all the time. And that's what I was doing. I worked in Germany. I even worked in a bierstube in Germany. And I was also working-- when I was working in Germany, I was working with Marek Weber. Marek Weber was the-- he was a Jewish man. He-- he came to America just before-- well, he was one of the guys that got out. And he-- I worked with him. He was the [GERMAN]. He was the Waltz King. And I was playing violin with him. I worked in Italy. I started in-- I went from London, [BACKGROUND NOISE] arrived in Italy on the 3rd of November, and opened up on November the 4th, which is the Italian Armistice Day. Opened up at the Hollywood Club in the Palace Ambassador's Hotel, which is in the Via Veneto in Rome. That was in 1937.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a contract, you know. We finished this contract. And then I was-- I was asked by Teddy [? Schtauffa ?] who was also working in Rome at the time, would you like to come and join us? We're going to work at the Imperial Hotel in Vienna, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the Imperial Hotel in Vienna, for people who know Vienna, is in the Ringstrasse facing the [NON-ENGLISH] and also St. Stephansplatz, which is that big cathedral with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH] on top of the roof. Anybody that knows that will tell you that this is culture. And we started on March the 1st, 1938. Things were-- you know, we felt something. There was a kind of atmosphere, but we couldn't place ourselves with it. Then, on Friday morning, March the 13th, there was a whole tumult. And early in the morning, that's when the Anschluss started. The Germans marched in. And everybody was up like this, Heil Hitler, you know. They paid us off for the contract. The contract-- we were booked for six months. They paid us for the whole lot. Being British, they didn't want no business, you know. And I came back to England. When I came back to England, I came and saw my mother. I said, mama [INAUDIBLE], buy whatever you can. [NON-ENGLISH]. There's going to be a war. Because I've seen it in Italy happening. And I see it now in Germany. Buy what you can, but [NON-ENGLISH] in the garden. But buy, because it's going to be a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember? Is there anything specific you remember about the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- we went up on the roof to watch it at the Imperial Hotel-- the whole band. And they were very, very-- you know. Because Teddy [? Schtauffa ?] and the others-- I don't know where they went after that. I got out. I came back to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the-- the German army rolling in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course you did. With the-- with the Panzer, with-- with everything. And all the people were like this, you know, lining the streets. Anyhow, from then, I came home. And we were working in England. And then came a time, when-- the morning of September the 3rd-- I was, at that time, working at the Mirabell Restaurant in Curzon Street, which is in Mayfair, London. And at that time, Sunday, September the 3rd, Chamberlain came on and said that so and so, and so and so, as from 11 o'clock this morning, we are now at war with-- with the Germany. I immediately went to see my parents. And my father said to us-- my two brothers, who you see the picture up there-- said, we have found our haven here. We're lucky to be here. We don't know what's going to happen. Because he hadn't invaded Poland at that time. Yes, he had! Yes. He-- he had invaded Poland, and Britain said, either you get out, or we're at war. Yes. Pardon me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in September '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: September the 3rd, 1939. During that time-- and then Italy came into the war in July of '40, after Dunkirk. Dunkirk happened on the 6th of June. Now, just before the 6th of June, I was working with Jack Buchanan. And in the same as the USO, it was called ENSA, Entertainment National Service Administration. And we were in Reims, in France. Epernay, Reims-- you know, that's the champagne district. We were going around entertaining the British troops, because at that time, it was a phony war. And everybody was singing you know. And it was on that Friday morning, all at once, the bombs started to fall. And we were told to get out, because we had women with us in the show and everything, you see. That was on May the 10th. Friday, May the 10th. Like today, is May the 10th. This is the anniversary day of that thing. They put us in a coach, and they said, don't stop for anything. Go to the Port of Calais. After a while, we got to the Port of Calais, and there were British soldiers that were supposed to be going on leave, but they were turning them back. They were turning them back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got back to England. And my wife, when I walked in, she looked at me and she said, what's the matter with you? I said, hellzapoppin'. Because at that time, there was a comedian in America that was doing a show called Hellzapoppin'. I said hell-- and she started to laugh, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, I kept on working. And I was still at the Mirabell when I was called up. And I was called up to the 50th-- I was called up to the 57th Training Regiment, Royal Armoured-- Royal Tank Corps, which later became the Royal Armoured Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had a-- a draft set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INTERPOSING VOICES] The draft was set up that every week in the newspaper it appeared that those born on so-and-so date, and so-and-so date, and so-and-so date register at the post office. And when it came my time, I registered at the post office. But they usually waited till a week after your birthday. So I was called into the army on September the 18th, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: 29. And I went through training, but when they found out that I was a professional musician and who I'd worked with, and what I did-- the band had suffered terribly in the evacuation of-- evacuation of Greece, at that time, when Greece fell, you know-- that most of the musicians that were in the band were stretcher bearers. And they had been taken prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the band, the nucleus, had to be made up again. And I went straight from the drill, from being drilled, you know, on the barracks square, I went straight into the band, which is a very cushy job. And we had a band master, Reg Ridewood, his name was, an absolute gentleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did very well. We were now-- and our-- our-- the colonel in charge, the lieutenant colonel in charge of our regiment was Colonel Broome. Spelled B-R-O-O-M-E. And he was glad that he now had an orchestra, a-- a band, to play for parades.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. And we still played for parades. Unfortunately, Reg Ridewood died from meningitis very early in 1941. And they sent us a new band leader by the name of Jaeger. A mumser like you never heard in your-- met in your life. And he was so proud of his German heritage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, every Sunday, we were required to play for church service. And it was supposed to be non-denominational. And one day, I had a row with this guy because I knew he was yiddified. You know what I mean, yiddified? Uh-uh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an English expression when somebody's anti-Jewish. They say he's yiddified. And so I requested that I don't do church service, because I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, you won't do church service because you're a Jew? OK. But you'll stand outside the gymnasium while the men are inside. You'll stand at attention outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was [INAUDIBLE], and I stood at attention. One of the first people to come out was the RSM. That's the regimental sergeant major, whose name was Thompson.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what are you doing out here standing at attention? And I told him. Just then the band master came out. He says, what do you-- and the band master said, he's one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hit him. And he went down. And I hit him real good. The next thing I know, I'm whisked off, come in front of the-- the colonel, and it all came out. And the colonel said my reactions, under the circumstances, can be, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Understandable.

speaker: interviewee
text: --understood. Understood. And he released me from that. And told the band master to stay-- to stay, because he gave him a real rucking. He really gave him a going over for doing that, for asking me to stand at attention, which he had no right to do, to punish me for not doing that. Anyhow, the first thing I did, I asked the colonel, I want to be returned to duty. I was returned to duty. I was returned to a regiment, out of the band, finished. And I was returned to the regiment, which-- I then went under thorough training. I was gun loader, a gun loader wireless operator-- wireless radio operator. I was a gun loader wireless operator. And of course, you had to do all the things in case one got shot. And then, just-- well, it was-- when America came into the war on December the 7th, and stuff started to boil, because we were taking a belting from Rommel.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in-- in one minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
